ops: extend nix dev shell with ops validation tools
description:
## Context
`shell.nix` currently ships dotnet/graphics/python deps for building the game. Ops scripts and subagents still `apt-get install shellcheck` etc. mid-run because the flake doesn't expose ops validation tools. Pin them in nix so everyone gets the same versions, and subagents can drop the ad-hoc apt calls.

## Task
Add ops validation tools to the `dependencies` list in `shell.nix`. Scope: tools that validate committed artifacts (prometheus/loki configs, shell scripts, yaml, systemd units).

## Scope — add to dependencies
- `shellcheck` — shell script linter (used by setup.postgres.sh, setup.watchdog.sh, backup.sh, etc.)
- `yamllint` — YAML linter (ops/observability/*.yml, docker-compose.yml, appsettings.yml.example)
- `prometheus` — provides `promtool` for `promtool check config ops/observability/prometheus.yml`
- `grafana-loki` or `loki` (whichever package name nixpkgs uses) — provides `loki -verify-config` and `logcli`
- `grafana` — provides `grafana-cli` for datasource/dashboard validation
- `systemd` — provides `systemd-analyze verify` (may already be present on the host; adding anyway for reproducibility; confirm it works inside `nix develop` on Linux)
- `go-task` / `just` — **skip** unless you already see one used; don't add speculatively

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `shell.nix` `dependencies` list extended; keep existing entries intact
- [ ] `nix-instantiate --parse shell.nix > /dev/null` succeeds (syntax)
- [ ] `nix develop -c which shellcheck promtool yamllint loki grafana-cli` all resolve to /nix/store paths (run only if you have confirmed nix daemon is available in the worktree env; otherwise document the validation path taken)
- [ ] `flake.lock` regenerated if changed (shell.nix change shouldn't bump it unless you touched flake.nix)
- [ ] Update `CLAUDE.md` "Nix env in worktree subagents" section to note that these tools are now pinned — so subagents can rely on them being present without `apt-get install`
- [ ] One commit with `Bead: vs-2ti` trailer, gitmoji `:wrench:`

## Out of scope
- Do NOT add services-flake or process-compose (that's vs-1bb)
- Do NOT modify flake.nix unless absolutely required (shell.nix edit should suffice)
- Do NOT run `nix build` or heavy evaluations that could take minutes

## Notes for the subagent
- Check nixpkgs package names exact match: `grafana-loki` vs `loki` — on recent nixpkgs it's just `grafana-loki`. Use `nix search nixpkgs <name>` if unsure (may be slow/offline; if so, pick the name that matches the nixpkgs release-25.11 branch — you can WebFetch https://search.nixos.org/packages?channel=25.11&query=<name>).
- The shell.nix uses `pkgs` from a channel-pinned nixpkgs. Trust the channel's package naming.

title: ops: front ss14-watchdog admin api with caddy + https (lock down :5000)
description:
## Context
During vs-1np/vs-25k bring-up we had to bind the SS14.Watchdog admin API
publicly (`Urls: http://0.0.0.0:5000`) and open ufw port 5000 so the SS14
client could download `SS14.Client.zip` from the watchdog's Local update
provider. The provider always sets the cvar
`build.download_url=http://<BaseUrl>/instances/<instance>/binaries/SS14.Client.zip`
and the client needs to reach that URL.

Current state on zig-computer:
- `/opt/ss14-watchdog/appsettings.yml` has
    - `BaseUrl: http://51.81.33.136:5000/`
    - `Urls: http://0.0.0.0:5000`
- `ufw allow 5000/tcp` (wide open)
- Watchdog admin endpoints (restart/update/stop/etc.) are reachable from
  the public internet, gated only by HTTP Basic auth with the generated
  ApiToken. A network observer between a client and the VPS can capture
  the token in plaintext.

This is fine for day-one testing but needs to close before anyone
important uses the server.

## Task

Front the watchdog with Caddy on HTTPS. Two target shapes — pick one:

### Option A: split domain
- `admin.vacation.example.com` → Caddy → `127.0.0.1:5000` (full watchdog API)
- `download.vacation.example.com/client.zip` → Caddy → `127.0.0.1:5000/instances/vacation-station/binaries/SS14.Client.zip` (public download only)
- Watchdog bind back to `127.0.0.1:5000`, ufw closes :5000 public

### Option B: one domain, path-based
- `vacation.example.com/client.zip` → Caddy → watchdog binaries endpoint
- `vacation.example.com/admin/*` → Caddy + basic auth passthrough → watchdog
- Same binding + ufw cleanup

Prefer A — cleaner ACL, less path rewriting. docs/NETWORKING.md already
has the pattern for Grafana; extend it.

## Changes required

1. **Pick a domain / subdomain** and DNS-point it at 51.81.33.136.
2. **Caddyfile** block(s) under `/etc/caddy/Caddyfile`:
   ```
   download.vacation.example.com {
       @clientzip path /client.zip
       handle @clientzip {
           reverse_proxy 127.0.0.1:5000 {
               rewrite /instances/vacation-station/binaries/SS14.Client.zip
           }
       }
   }
   admin.vacation.example.com {
       basicauth {
           # optional extra layer if you want; watchdog does its own auth
       }
       reverse_proxy 127.0.0.1:5000
   }
   ```
3. **Update watchdog appsettings.yml**:
   - `BaseUrl: https://download.vacation.example.com/` — what the game server
     advertises in `/info.download_url`
   - `Urls: http://127.0.0.1:5000` — bind back to loopback
4. **ufw**: `sudo ufw delete allow 5000/tcp` (Caddy 80/443 are public, watchdog isn't)
5. **Restart**:
   - `sudo systemctl reload caddy`
   - `sudo systemctl restart ss14-watchdog`
   - POST /instances/vacation-station/restart to force game server to pick up new download_url
6. **Verify**:
   - `curl -I https://download.vacation.example.com/client.zip` → 200 + valid TLS
   - `curl https://51.81.33.136:1212/info | jq .build.download_url` → matches the new HTTPS URL
   - `ss -tln | grep 5000` shows `127.0.0.1:5000` only (not 0.0.0.0)
   - Connect via SS14 launcher again, confirm still works
7. **docs/OPERATIONS.md**: add a "Watchdog behind Caddy" subsection
   alongside the existing Grafana Caddy docs.
8. **docs/NETWORKING.md**: add the download.vacation / admin.vacation blocks.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Watchdog bound to 127.0.0.1 only; ufw :5000 rule removed
- [ ] TLS cert valid for the chosen domain (Caddy auto-acme)
- [ ] Client download URL served over HTTPS
- [ ] ApiToken never crosses plaintext TCP again (any admin ops go via HTTPS)
- [ ] SS14 launcher connects + downloads client successfully
- [ ] docs updated (OPERATIONS + NETWORKING)
- [ ] record completion timestamp before close

## Out of scope
- Don't migrate UpdateType from Local to Manifest (separate bead — that's a
  real CI/CD change)
- Don't add OIDC / SSO in front of the watchdog — ApiToken alone is fine
  once the wire is encrypted
- Don't change the ApiToken itself (rotation is a separate runbook)

## Notes
- Caddy auto-acme requires ports 80 and 443 open on the cloud firewall
  (already required for any HTTPS setup).
- Watchdog's BaseUrl value is what ends up in the game server's `/info`
  response and ultimately in the client launcher's download request.
  Must match the Caddy-fronted public hostname exactly.

title: fix: vs-1bb dev services stack — prometheus config dup, postgres init order, grafana plugin noise
description:
## Context
Smoke-tested `nix run .#dev-services` for the first time. Stack evaluates and Loki+Grafana boot cleanly, but Prometheus and Postgres don't come up. All findings are in the vs-1bb dev flake definition — prod docker-compose is unaffected.

## Three bugs (two blocking, one cosmetic)

### Bug 1 (blocking) — Prometheus: duplicate `--config.file` flag
`flake.nix` passes `--config.file=${prometheusConfigDev}` via `extraFlags` to services-flake's prometheus module. The module already generates a `--config.file` arg internally, and Prometheus CLI parsing rejects the duplicate:
```
[prom1] Error parsing command line arguments: flag 'config.file' cannot be repeated
```

**Fix direction:** instead of extraFlags, feed the dev-overlay YAML to services-flake the "right" way:
- The services-flake prometheus module typically exposes an `extraConfig` option (raw YAML string merged into the generated config) or an `externalUrl`/`configFile` direct path. Read `/nix/store/.../services-flake/nix/services/prometheus.nix` to find the real interface.
- If the module has `configFile` as an option (not just extraFlags), point it at our dev-overlay derivation.
- If the module only offers `extraConfig` (string-merge), translate our `ops/observability/prometheus.yml` into the module's expected attrset form. May need to feed `global`, `scrape_configs`, `external_labels` as nix attrs rather than a raw file.
- Last resort: wrap `pkgs.prometheus` in a `writeShellScriptBin` that ignores the module's auto-generated flag and takes ours, then point `package` at the wrapper.

### Bug 2 (blocking) — Postgres: initialScript ordering
Log shows init runs `initialScript.before` (our SQL: `CREATE USER vs14 ...; ALTER DATABASE vacation_station OWNER TO vs14;`) BEFORE `initialDatabases` creates the `vacation_station` DB:
```
[pg1-init] CREATE ROLE
[pg1-init] ERROR:  database "vacation_station" does not exist
[pg1-init] STATEMENT:  ALTER DATABASE vacation_station OWNER TO vs14;
```
Whole service then teardown.

**Fix direction:**
- Move the `ALTER DATABASE ... OWNER TO vs14` to `initialScript.after` (runs after initialDatabases).
- OR set the owner at db-creation time via `initialDatabases = [{ name = "vacation_station"; }]` plus an extra `initialScript.after` that does the alter, OR use whatever richer syntax services-flake exposes for owner assignment.
- Check the module source for the exact hook names; `initialScript` may have `before`/`after` sub-attrs or it may be a single script where we should order the statements relative to initialDatabases ourselves.

### Bug 3 (cosmetic) — Grafana plugin install noise
Grafana 12.3.0 ships `grafana-lokiexplore-app` and `grafana-pyroscope-app` in its default auto-install list. On startup it tries to install them from grafana.com into `/nix/store/.../share/grafana/data/plugins/` which is read-only:
```
error="failed to extract plugin archive: could not create ..., permission denied"
```
Non-fatal — Grafana still serves. Just spam in the log.

**Fix direction:** disable the auto-install. Either:
- Set `services.grafana.graf1.extraConf.plugins.plugin_admin_enabled = false` or similar (read module options)
- Explicitly set the grafana option that controls which plugins auto-install to an empty list (look for `auto_updates`, `plugin_admin_enabled`, or `default_plugins` in grafana's ini settings)
- Override `GF_INSTALL_PLUGINS=""` if the module exposes env injection

## Validation
Prepend `eval "$(direnv export bash)" 2>/dev/null;` as usual.

1. `nix flake check --no-build` — still passes
2. Boot the stack in no-TUI mode:
   ```
   rm -rf .data
   nix run .#dev-services -- --tui=false > /tmp/vs14-stack.log 2>&1 &
   sleep 45
   ```
3. Probe health:
   ```
   curl -sS localhost:9090/-/healthy     # expect 200
   curl -sS localhost:3100/ready          # expect 200 "ready"
   curl -sS localhost:3000/api/health     # expect 200
   PGPASSWORD=dev-only-insecure psql -h localhost -U vs14 -d vacation_station -c 'select 1'  # expect "1"
   ```
4. Confirm Grafana log no longer shows plugin-install permission errors
5. Kill stack: `pkill -f process-compose` then `rm -rf .data`

## Out of scope
- Do NOT add/remove services in the stack (keep just postgres/prom/loki/grafana)
- Do NOT change prod docker-compose / prometheus.yml / loki-config.yml / provisioning YAMLs — this is pure dev-flake fix
- Do NOT change shell.nix

## Notes for the subagent
- services-flake source on the current system (find via `find $(nix-store --realise $(nix eval --raw .#inputs.services-flake.outPath)) -name '*.nix'` or `nix eval --raw .#inputs.services-flake.outPath` then `ls` that path)
- Prometheus version in use: check `nix run .#dev-services -- --help` or the extraFlags log to confirm what we're passing against
- Postgres version is 17.7 per smoke test log

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `nix flake check --no-build` passes
- [ ] `curl localhost:9090/-/healthy` returns 200 after stack boot
- [ ] `psql -h localhost -U vs14 -d vacation_station -c 'select 1'` connects and returns 1 (with PGPASSWORD=dev-only-insecure)
- [ ] `curl localhost:3100/ready` returns 200 (unchanged from current state)
- [ ] `curl localhost:3000/api/health` returns 200 (unchanged from current state)
- [ ] Grafana log shows no "Failed to install plugin" permission errors
- [ ] Single commit with `Bead: vs-2yf` trailer, `:bug:` gitmoji

## Commit example
```
:bug: nix: fix dev services stack boot issues

- Prometheus: replace extraFlags --config.file hack with services-flake's
  native configFile/extraConfig interface; stops duplicate-flag rejection
- Postgres: move ALTER DATABASE OWNER into initialScript.after so the DB
  created by initialDatabases exists before we try to reassign it
- Grafana: disable auto-install of baked-in plugins (lokiexplore, pyroscope)
  that fail with permission-denied writing to the read-only nix-store
  package dir

Smoke-tested: all four endpoints return 200 / serve queries.

Bead: vs-2yf
Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
```

ops: cross-platform nix dev shell (darwin + windows via wsl2)
description:
## Context
The nix flake (`flake.nix`) currently restricts to `x86_64-linux` + `aarch64-linux`. `shell.nix` pulls libdrm/mesa/xorg/gtk3 — all Linux-only — which causes `nix flake check` to fail on darwin. Windows lacks native nix entirely; any nix-based dev path must go through WSL2.

Goal: every platform gets a usable nix-first dev experience, aligned with the first-class nix stance.

## Task

### macOS (darwin): real flake support
1. Split `shell.nix` into platform-aware structure. Options:
   - (a) `shell.nix` takes a `pkgs` arg, branches on `pkgs.stdenv.isDarwin` / `isLinux`, conditionally includes graphics libs.
   - (b) Factor out `shell/common.nix`, `shell/linux.nix`, `shell/darwin.nix`; `shell.nix` selects based on system.
   Prefer (a) — less file sprawl. The branching is `lib.optionals pkgs.stdenv.isDarwin [...] ++ lib.optionals pkgs.stdenv.isLinux [...]`.
2. Darwin-specific deps: `dotnet-sdk_10` still needed. Skip all X11 / mesa / gtk / libdrm / alsa-lib / wayland — they don't exist on darwin. Graphics on mac goes via Apple frameworks, which Robust/RobustToolbox picks up at runtime if it's built to target darwin. Most of those libs may not be needed at all for a SERVER-ONLY darwin dev shell.
3. Decide scope: **server-only** on darwin (no client deps), or attempt client support. Server-only is much simpler and matches the realistic contributor path (client dev on mac needs a proper Mac build of the engine which is a separate problem). Pick server-only and document that mac contributors who need client rendering should use a Linux VM.
4. Re-enable darwin systems in `flake.nix`: `systems = inputs.flake-parts.lib.systems.flakeExposed;` or the explicit list `[ "x86_64-linux" "aarch64-linux" "x86_64-darwin" "aarch64-darwin" ]`.
5. Verify: `nix flake check --no-build --all-systems` passes (evaluates all four systems; darwin devShell pulls fewer deps).

### Windows: WSL2 documentation path
6. No flake changes for Windows — it has no native nix. Windows contributors install WSL2 with Ubuntu, then clone the repo inside WSL and use the standard Linux nix path.
7. Add a "Windows (via WSL2)" subsection to `docs/DEVELOPMENT.md`:
   - Install WSL2: `wsl --install -d Ubuntu-24.04`
   - Inside WSL: install nix (single-user install from https://nixos.org/download), enable flakes, install direnv
   - Clone repo inside WSL filesystem (NOT on Windows drive `/mnt/c/...` — perf disaster)
   - `direnv allow` + standard dev flow
   - Client dev from WSL: GUI apps work via WSLg (Win11+); for audio + OpenGL, this is experimental and may not work reliably. Server-only from WSL is solid.
   - Point macOS contributors who don't want a VM to a similar "server-only nix dev" experience.

### Docs + skill update
8. Update `docs/DEVELOPMENT.md`:
   - Quick Start (Nix) section: mention all three platforms (Linux native, macOS native server-only, Windows via WSL2)
   - Add Windows (via WSL2) subsection
   - Note macOS server-only scope
9. Update `.claude/skills/nix/SKILL.md`:
   - Replace the "Flake is Linux-only" warning with an up-to-date matrix:
     | Platform | Native nix | Client shell | Server shell |
     | Linux    | ✅         | ✅           | ✅           |
     | macOS    | ✅         | ❌ (use VM)  | ✅           |
     | Windows  | ❌ use WSL | ❌           | ✅           |
   - Update the "When to use which path" table accordingly
10. Update `CLAUDE.md` Dev Environment section to remove the Linux-only claim and point at `docs/DEVELOPMENT.md` for platform specifics.

## Out of scope
- Do not attempt to get the full client stack (graphics, audio) working on darwin or WSLg — deep rabbit hole. Document the limitation and move on.
- Do not modify anything outside `shell.nix`, `flake.nix`, docs, and the /nix skill.
- Do not bump `shell.nix` dependency versions beyond what's needed to get darwin to evaluate.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `shell.nix` branches cleanly on `stdenv.isDarwin` / `isLinux`; existing Linux entries unchanged
- [ ] `flake.nix` systems list includes all four (`x86_64-linux`, `aarch64-linux`, `x86_64-darwin`, `aarch64-darwin`)
- [ ] `nix flake check --no-build --all-systems` passes on a Linux host (you may lack a darwin builder — evaluation alone should succeed because flake-parts doesn't require a remote builder for `check`)
- [ ] `docs/DEVELOPMENT.md` has a Windows (WSL2) subsection and updated platform framing
- [ ] `.claude/skills/nix/SKILL.md` has an updated platform support matrix
- [ ] `CLAUDE.md` reflects cross-platform support
- [ ] Single commit, `Bead: vs-35q` trailer, gitmoji `:sparkles:` or `:construction_worker:`

## Notes
- Windows-via-WSL2 is not really "Windows support" — it's "Linux nix running inside a Windows environment." That's fine and honest; just document clearly.
- Darwin users running this on Apple Silicon should get `aarch64-darwin` automatically.
- If `nix flake check` hits a darwin-specific missing dep we can't resolve, it's OK to conditionally exclude darwin from one specific output (e.g. drop `dev-services` on darwin if services-flake has Linux-only assumptions). Document any such exclusions in the matrix.

title: ci: build-docfx.yml fails at engine submodule update
description:
## Context
`.github/workflows/build-docfx.yml` runs on schedule (`0 0 * * 0`, weekly
Sunday midnight). Latest run: **failure at step "Update Engine Submodules"**.
Subsequent steps (dotnet, build, publish docfx) all skipped.

## Probable root cause
The workflow does `git submodule update --init --recursive` then runs
custom engine-update shell. The fork only just got the RobustToolbox
gitlink committed (commit `d80851a3b` during vs-25z). The failure
pre-dates that fix and was running against a repo where the gitlink
didn't exist.

Also: publish step pushes generated docs to GitHub Pages via a PAT
(`secrets.GITHUB_PAT_DOCFX` or similar) — we likely don't have that
secret configured, so even if submodule update succeeded, publish would
fail at the PAT check.

## Investigation needed
1. Confirm whether the next scheduled run (2026-04-19 00:00 UTC)
   succeeds now that the gitlink is committed — if yes, close as fixed.
2. If still failing, inspect the step output — might be one of:
   - Missing engine-update remote / branch
   - Missing GitHub Pages publish secret
   - The workflow hardcodes upstream-specific paths
3. Decide whether we even want auto-published docfx on our fork. We
   don't have a docs site today; this was upstream's pipeline.

## Options
### Option A: disable (like labeler-review)
If we don't serve docfx-generated docs, this is pure upstream noise.
Disable via `gh api .../disable`.

### Option B: fix the secret + submodule path for our fork
Set up GH Pages on `azigler.github.io/vacation-station-14`, create the
PAT, uncomment or add a proper repository guard. Unused today so
mostly busywork.

### Option C: guard with `if: github.repository ==`
Skip on fork, preserve for upstream-sync.

## Recommendation
**A** (disable) for now. If we ever want a docfx site later, we can
revisit — it's a separate decision from "should this workflow run."

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Decision made + recorded
- [ ] If disable: `gh api -X PUT .../disable`, note the workflow id
- [ ] If fix: next scheduled run succeeds, docs live somewhere
- [ ] If guard: fork-case skips cleanly, no failures

title: ci: update-credits.yml skips on fork by design
description:
## Context
`.github/workflows/update-credits.yml` runs on schedule (weekly Sunday
midnight). Latest run: **skipped**. This is NOT a failure — the workflow
has `if: github.repository == 'DeltaV-Station/Delta-v'`, so every run on
our fork correctly skips the job.

Upstream even left a comment in the file:
```
# Hey there fork dev! If you like to include your own contributors in
# this then you can probably just change this to your own repo
# Do this in dump_github_contributors.ps1 too into your own repo
```

## Options
### Option A: leave it (no action)
Harmless skips every Sunday. Doesn't pollute the failures list. Does
generate a run entry in GitHub Actions history but that's minor.

### Option B: rewrite for VS14 contributors
Flip the guard to our repo, adapt `dump_github_contributors.ps1` to
point at azigler/vacation-station-14. On success, appends new
contributors to our credits/patreons file weekly. Low-value until we
have meaningful contributor traffic.

### Option C: delete
If we're not going to run it, removing reduces workflow file count and
prevents confusion. On upstream-sync, we'd re-delete.

## Recommendation
**A** (leave). Zero cost, zero harm, preserved for future activation.
When we have a few outside contributors and want an auto-updating
credits file, flip to B with a one-line change to the guard + a script
tweak.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Decision recorded
- [ ] If leave: close the bead with "no action, leaving as documented
      skip"
- [ ] If rewrite: guard flipped, ps1 adapted, first run produces a PR
      or commit with current contributors
- [ ] If delete: file removed + added to upstream-sync drop list

title: human: write rules, MOTD, server positioning, website copy, discord onboarding
description:
## Context
Hub launch, Discord bootstrap, and the website all depend on written
content — the server's identity in words. None of this is code; all of
it is YOUR voice. Can't automate what VS14 stands for.

Currently the committed config.toml.example has placeholder MOTD and no
rules. Discord has no welcome text. Website has no copy. Game MOTD is
blank or upstream default.

Each piece of copy has downstream consumers. Writing in one place and
distributing to all three (game / Discord / website) is the goal (single
source of truth — see vs-2l2 Phase 5 and vs-2dr integrations).

## Deliverables

### 1. Positioning statement (one paragraph)
The 30-second pitch. Goes on:
- Website hero
- Discord `#welcome`
- Hub tags + server description
- `ss14://` server browser blurb

Answer these in order:
- What genre of SS14 server is VS14? (mid-RP? low-RP with ambition?
  PvP-oriented? story-first?)
- What audience? (Veterans burnt out on upstream? Newbies? Delta-V
  refugees?)
- What ONE thing differentiates VS14 from the 50+ other SS14 servers?
- What will you NOT do? (No ERP. No hardcore-RP requirements. Whatever
  the negative-space is.)

Keep under 60 words for the short version; expand to 3 paragraphs for
the about page.

### 2. Server rules (markdown, `docs/community/rules.md`)
Structure (common SS14 pattern, adapt to VS14 voice):
1. **Meta rules** — no ban evasion, no harassment, follow admin
   instructions, how to appeal
2. **Roleplay expectations** — character vs player, IC/OOC separation,
   expected engagement level
3. **Conduct** — no slurs, no bigotry, how to handle conflict
4. **Antag behavior** — acceptable antag play, what crosses the line
5. **Admin escalation** — how to get an admin, what they can do, what
   they won't do
6. **Grief / self-antag** — definitions, consequences
7. **Out-of-server rules** — Discord rules apply equally; community
   extends across both
8. **Metagaming / exploiting** — bugs, client tweaks, third-party tools
9. **Account / verification** — Wizden account expectations

Look at upstream SS14 rules + Delta-V rules + three other good community
servers. Adapt, don't copy. Ban list cites rule numbers, so numbering
matters.

### 3. MOTD (short, `docs/community/motd.md`)
What players see the moment they connect. ~2-4 lines. Usually:
- Server name + fork lineage
- Link to rules
- Link to Discord
- Optional flavor line

Example shape:
```
Welcome to Vacation Station 14 — a hard fork of Delta-V.
Rules: vacation.yourdomain.com/rules | Discord: discord.gg/xxx
Have fun, respect shift structure, break character only in OOC. o7
```

### 4. Website home hero + about page (`web/content/`)
- Hero: 1-sentence pitch + CTA button ("Connect" / "Download launcher")
- About: 3-4 paragraphs — what/who/why, fork lineage, license, how to
  contribute
- Rules page: rendered from `docs/community/rules.md`
- Connect page: launcher download instructions + troubleshooting

### 5. Discord onboarding
- Server description (the short positioning statement)
- `#welcome` pinned message with rules summary + connect instructions
- Rules channel content (rendered from same source as website /rules)
- Role descriptions (what @Admin does, what @Verified is for, etc.)
- Welcome DM to new joiners

### 6. Launch announcement copy
For when you flip the hub advertise switch (vs-17n):
- Tweet / Mastodon / bluesky post
- Discord announcement (both in VS14's own server AND — if you're
  comfortable — in upstream SS14 community spaces)
- Reddit /r/ss14 post (if appropriate)
- Email / DM to Delta-V and other sibling communities for cross-promotion

## Single-source-of-truth layout
```
docs/community/
├── positioning.md          short + long versions
├── rules.md                numbered rules, canonical
├── motd.md                 compressed in-game welcome
├── about.md                 website about-page source
├── launch-announcement.md  copy drafts for launch day
└── discord/
    ├── welcome.md
    ├── roles.md
    └── rules-channel.md    (often the same as rules.md with Discord
                             formatting tweaks)
```

Consumers:
- Game server: deploys `rules.md` + `motd.md` into config.toml cvars
- Discord bot: reads from `docs/community/` at runtime
- Website: imports from `docs/community/` at build time

Edit once, ship three places.

## Writing guidance (not rules — just what tends to work)
- Avoid upstream's passive "X is not allowed"; prefer VS14's voice
  (first-person community voice — "we..." / "here we...")
- Rules should be enforceable; vague rules create drama
- Positioning should close doors ("we are NOT X") as much as open them
- Shorter MOTD is better than longer; players skim
- Humor carefully — what reads as charming to you may read as
  off-putting to a first-timer

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `docs/community/positioning.md` — short + long versions written
- [ ] `docs/community/rules.md` — numbered, ≥ 8 sections, enforceable
- [ ] `docs/community/motd.md` — 2-4 lines
- [ ] `docs/community/about.md` — website about page
- [ ] `docs/community/discord/welcome.md` + `roles.md` + `rules-channel.md`
- [ ] `docs/community/launch-announcement.md` — copy for social posts
- [ ] All six consumed by at least one downstream service (once those
      services exist)
- [ ] First proofread pass — fresh eyes read it, flag anything weird

## Out of scope
- Visual brand (logo, color palette, fonts) — separate bead, pairs
  with vs-2dr website Phase 1 styling
- Translated copies (localization) — deferred until we have non-EN
  players asking
- Legal boilerplate (TOS, privacy) — separate bead if we start
  collecting accounts / donations

## Dependencies
None — this is writing. Blocks vs-17n, vs-2l2 Phase 3, vs-2dr Phase 2.

title: fix: grafana postgres datasource type mismatch across prod + dev
description:
## Context
vs-2p3 + vs-13x shipped conflicting datasource types for Postgres in Grafana:

- `ops/observability/docker-compose.yml:107` env `GF_INSTALL_PLUGINS: grafana-postgresql-datasource` — installs the plugin
- `ops/observability/grafana/provisioning/datasources/datasources.yml:29` — provisions datasource with `type: postgres` (built-in)
- `ops/observability/grafana/dashboards/game-servers.json:41` (and panels in perf-metrics.json) — references `"type": "grafana-postgresql-datasource"` (plugin)

When Grafana provisions a datasource with `type: postgres` but a panel asks for `type: grafana-postgresql-datasource`, the panel fails to locate a matching datasource. In the dev services-flake stack the plugin isn't installed at all — no fallback there either.

This will silently break the Connections panel on Game Servers and any postgres-querying panel on Perf Metrics when the operator runs vs-205 verification.

## Task
Pick ONE datasource type and make all three surfaces agree. Recommend `grafana-postgresql-datasource` (the plugin) for parity with upstream SS14 dashboards — postgres-as-plugin offers query builder + TimescaleDB hooks the built-in lacks, and upstream dashboards were authored against it.

### Changes required

1. **`ops/observability/grafana/provisioning/datasources/datasources.yml`**: change Postgres block `type: postgres` → `type: grafana-postgresql-datasource`. Verify the rest of the block (jsonData, secureJsonData) is still compatible with the plugin schema (it should be — the plugin is a drop-in successor).

2. **`ops/observability/docker-compose.yml`**: confirm `GF_INSTALL_PLUGINS: grafana-postgresql-datasource` is correctly spelled and the plugin installs on grafana 11.3.0 startup. If the plugin requires a specific minimum grafana version, document it. No code change needed unless the image pin is too old.

3. **Dashboards** (`game-servers.json`, `perf-metrics.json`): already reference `grafana-postgresql-datasource` — no change needed. Do grep-verify that NO panel still references `type: postgres` by mistake; if any do, fix.

4. **Dev services-flake stack** (`flake.nix` `perSystem.process-compose."dev-services".services.grafana.graf1`): the plugin isn't in nixpkgs `grafanaPlugins`. Two options:
   - **(a) Install at runtime**: add `GF_INSTALL_PLUGINS=grafana-postgresql-datasource` as an env var / arg to the dev grafana so it fetches the plugin from grafana.com on first start. Services-flake grafana module may expose this via `services.grafana.graf1.settings.plugin_install` or `extraEnv`/`env`. Check the module.
   - **(b) Omit postgres in dev**: drop the Postgres datasource from the dev flake entirely and have contributors use Prometheus + Loki only in dev. Dashboards' postgres panels would show "no datasource" in dev but that's acceptable if documented.

   Prefer **(a)** if the services-flake grafana module supports runtime plugin install; otherwise fall back to **(b)** and document clearly in `docs/DEVELOPMENT.md` Dev Services Stack section.

5. **Cross-ref comment sync**: `ops/observability/grafana/provisioning/datasources/datasources.yml` and the dev flake's inlined grafana datasources should each carry a comment pointing at the other as the "also update me" sibling — reduces drift risk (finding #2 from vs-1bb).

### Validation
- `docker compose -f ops/observability/docker-compose.yml config` parses
- `python -c "import yaml; yaml.safe_load(open('ops/observability/grafana/provisioning/datasources/datasources.yml'))"` parses
- Each dashboard JSON still parses
- If option (a) chosen: `nix flake check --no-build --all-systems` passes
- Grep the repo for any remaining `type: postgres` references that should have been updated (and any `grafana-postgresql-datasource` that shouldn't have been touched — the dashboards are correct as-is)

### Commit
One commit, gitmoji `:bug:`, trailer `Bead: vs-3oe`.

## Out of scope
- Do not change image pins unless strictly required by plugin compatibility
- Do not rewrite the dashboard JSONs structurally (they're canonical as vs-13x landed them)
- Do not migrate off `grafana-postgresql-datasource` to another postgres backend

## References
- Plugin page: https://grafana.com/grafana/plugins/grafana-postgresql-datasource/
- services-flake grafana module: https://community.flake.parts/services-flake/services/grafana

title: ops: postgres setup from day 1
description:
## Context
Level 5 hosting uses PostgreSQL instead of the default SQLite. Required for
SS14.Admin (which doesn't support SQLite) and for multi-instance deployments.
We want Postgres from day 1 so DB schema is production-shaped.

SS14 server runs migrations automatically on startup — DB user just needs
to own its database.

## Task
Install PostgreSQL, create vacation_station DB and user, configure SS14 to use
Postgres instead of SQLite, verify migrations run cleanly on first server start.

## Acceptance Criteria

### Installation
- [ ] setup.postgres.sh script that installs postgresql-16 (or 17) via apt
- [ ] systemctl enable postgresql
- [ ] postgresql listening on localhost:5432 (default)

### Database setup
- [ ] DB user `vs14` created with generated password (stored in password manager)
- [ ] Database `vacation_station` created, owned by `vs14`
- [ ] Verified: `psql -U vs14 -d vacation_station -c "select 1"`

### SS14 config
- [ ] instances/vacation-station/config.toml includes:
  [database]
  engine = "postgres"
  pg_host = "localhost"
  pg_port = 5432
  pg_database = "vacation_station"
  pg_username = "vs14"
  pg_password = "..."   # from password manager, NOT in repo
- [ ] First server start runs migrations cleanly (check logs: migrations applied N)
- [ ] Verify tables created: `psql -U vs14 -d vacation_station -c "\dt"`

### Access control
- [ ] pg_hba.conf restricts connections to localhost (default in most distros)
- [ ] PostgreSQL NOT exposed publicly (port 5432 bound to localhost or firewalled)
- [ ] Password generated with cryptographic entropy (openssl rand, not a dictionary word)

### Backup
- [ ] Cron or systemd timer for `pg_dump -Fc` nightly
- [ ] Backup destination documented (local for now, object storage later)
- [ ] Retention policy documented (e.g. 7 daily, 4 weekly)

### Connection pooling
- [ ] Postgres max_connections reviewed (default 100 usually fine for single instance)
- [ ] No PgBouncer needed initially (revisit if we scale)

### Documentation
- [ ] docs/OPERATIONS.md Postgres section: install, user/DB creation, config wiring,
  backup/restore procedures, migration to S3-compatible storage path
- [ ] Credential rotation procedure documented

title: ops: add postgresql client to nix dev shell
description:
## Context
During vs-2yf smoke testing, the orchestrator had to invoke `psql` via `/nix/store/*postgresql*/bin/psql` because `psql` isn't on PATH in the `nix develop` shell. Quality-of-life gap — contributors validating DB work, running migrations by hand, or doing local debugging naturally reach for `psql`, `pg_dump`, and friends.

## Task
Add `postgresql` to `shell.nix` `commonDeps` (it's cross-platform, builds on Linux + darwin). The `postgresql` attr gives you `psql`, `pg_dump`, `pg_restore`, `createdb`, etc. on PATH.

**Important version matching**: the dev services-flake stack runs postgres 17.7 (observed in vs-1bb smoke test). Make sure the `postgresql` package we pull into the dev shell is version 17.x so wire-protocol and `pg_dump -Fc` archive format line up with the dev server. If nixpkgs release-25.11 defaults `postgresql` to a different major, explicitly pin `postgresql_17`.

## Validation
Prepend `eval "$(direnv export bash)" 2>/dev/null;` where needed.

1. `nix-instantiate --parse shell.nix` — OK
2. `nix develop -c psql --version` — reports 17.x
3. `nix develop -c which pg_dump` — resolves to a `/nix/store/...postgresql-17*/bin/pg_dump`
4. `nix flake check --no-build --all-systems` — still passes (darwin+linux)

Don't need to reboot the full services stack for this — a pure shell.nix change.

## Out of scope
- Don't add postgresql-server-only things or versioning infra
- Don't touch flake.nix
- Don't modify prod ops scripts (setup.postgres.sh already invokes psql via system path on the prod host)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `postgresql` (v17.x) in shell.nix commonDeps
- [ ] `psql --version` reports 17.x inside dev shell
- [ ] `nix flake check --no-build --all-systems` passes
- [ ] Single commit, `:wrench:` gitmoji, `Bead: vs-d7g` trailer

## Notes
If `postgresql_17` isn't available in nixpkgs release-25.11 but `postgresql` defaults to 16 or 18, document which version we got and flag it. A one-minor-version gap (16 client talking to 17 server) is fine for most client operations; a two-major gap is worth investigating.

title: ops: watchdog setup from day 1
description:
## Context
SS14.Watchdog (codename "Ian") runs the game server as a child process, handles
auto-updates, crash recovery, and exposes an admin API. Required for Level 4+
hosting. We want it from day 1.

Upstream: github.com/space-wizards/SS14.Watchdog
Docs: docs.spacestation14.com/en/server-hosting/setting-up-ss14-watchdog.html

## Dependencies
- Needs .NET 10 SDK + ASP.NET Core 10 Runtime installed (covered by setup.ubuntu.sh --server)

## Task
Build Watchdog from source, deploy as systemd service, configure a vacation-station
instance using the Manifest update provider (or Local initially until we publish
builds).

## Acceptance Criteria

### Installation
- [ ] setup.watchdog.sh script that clones, publishes, and installs to /opt/ss14-watchdog
- [ ] Publish command is exactly: `dotnet publish -c Release -r linux-x64 --no-self-contained`
  (variations break Mono.Posix.NETStandard.dll and chmod +x)
- [ ] Binary at /opt/ss14-watchdog/SS14.Watchdog

### systemd unit
- [ ] /etc/systemd/system/ss14-watchdog.service created
- [ ] KillMode=process (required if using PersistServers)
- [ ] OOMPolicy=continue (don't kill watchdog when game server OOMs)
- [ ] Environment="DOTNET_CLI_HOME=/tmp"
- [ ] Restart=on-failure
- [ ] systemctl enable --now ss14-watchdog

### appsettings.yml
- [ ] /opt/ss14-watchdog/appsettings.yml configured:
  - BaseUrl: http://localhost:5000/
  - Urls: http://*:5000 (or localhost-only + reverse proxy)
  - Serilog with Loki sink pointing at localhost:3100
  - Process.PersistServers: true
  - Notification.DiscordWebhook (optional, for crash alerts)
- [ ] Instance "vacation-station" configured with:
  - ApiToken (generated, NOT in repo — stored in password manager)
  - ApiPort: 1212 (matches game server [status] bind)
  - UpdateType: Local (initially) or Manifest (once we have a CDN)
  - TimeoutSeconds: 60
  - EnvironmentVariables: DOTNET_TieredPGO=1, DOTNET_TC_QuickJitForLoops=1, ROBUST_NUMERICS_AVX=true

### Instance data
- [ ] /opt/ss14-watchdog/instances/vacation-station/config.toml created
  (this is the GAME SERVER config — hostname, auth mode, metrics, loki, postgres)
- [ ] First build dropped into instances/vacation-station/binaries/ (for Local provider)

### Verification
- [ ] `systemctl is-active ss14-watchdog` returns "active"
- [ ] `curl http://localhost:5000/instances/vacation-station/ping` (or equivalent)
- [ ] Game server starts as child of Watchdog (verify with pstree/ps -ef)
- [ ] Crash recovery: `pkill -f Robust.Server`, confirm Watchdog respawns within timeout
- [ ] API auth test: `curl -u vacation-station:$TOKEN -X POST http://localhost:5000/instances/vacation-station/restart`

### Firewall
- [ ] Port 5000 localhost-only (or add to ufw + cloud firewall if remote control desired)
- [ ] Game server ports (UDP 1212, TCP 1212) open per docs/NETWORKING.md

### Documentation
- [ ] docs/OPERATIONS.md section on Watchdog (install, config, API, troubleshooting)
- [ ] API token storage procedure documented (password manager ref, never commit)
- [ ] Update provider migration path (Local -> Manifest when we have CDN)

title: ci: decide fate of disabled labeler-review.yml
description:
## Context
`.github/workflows/labeler-review.yml` was commented-out end-to-end in the
initial fork (every line begins with `#`) but GitHub still registered it as
an active workflow and generated a zero-job "failure" run on every push to
main. During vs-25k we disabled it via
`gh api -X PUT ... /actions/workflows/<id>/disable` to match Delta-V
upstream's state (`disabled_manually`).

That's a stop-gap, not a decision. The file on disk still carries a
leftover stub with upstream's note: "The PAT does not have the correct
permissions to make this work; Eventually we'll want to replace this
workflow with a proper bot."

## Task — pick one path, do the work

### Option A: delete entirely
Cleanest. No ghost workflow, no confusion. Downside: every upstream-sync
may re-introduce the file, forcing us to re-delete. Add to the `/upstream-sync`
skill's "always drop" list to automate.

### Option B: revive with a proper PAT
Upstream's plan. Create a PAT with `contents:read` + `pull-requests:write`
in a bot account, store as `LABELER_PAT` secret, uncomment the file, update
the `github.repository` check to `azigler/vacation-station-14`, adjust the
team membership check (`space-wizards/maintainers` → our equivalent or
deleted).

### Option C: replace with a label-on-approval GitHub App
The "proper bot" the upstream comment aspires to. More involved — needs a
hosted app (or paid service like Probot). Only worth it if labeling is
actually part of our workflow. We don't currently use `S: Approved` for
anything, so likely not.

## Recommendation
**A** (delete) unless we commit to using approval labels in our review flow.
vs-???'s decision should answer: do we want `S: Approved` as a trigger for
anything (auto-merge, deploy gates, etc.)? If no → delete. If yes → B or C.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Decision made + recorded in this bead
- [ ] If delete: file removed, upstream-sync skill updated to drop on pull
- [ ] If revive: PAT provisioned, secret added, repo guard updated,
      workflow re-enabled via API
- [ ] The existing `disabled_manually` state is either resolved (delete)
      or flipped back to `active` (revive)

title: research: adjacent SS14 services (watchdog, admin, cdn, etc)
human: run vs-3ty postgres setup on host
description:
## Context
vs-3ty shipped the artifacts (setup.postgres.sh, backup units, config template, ops docs) but the orchestrator's worktree subagent is sandboxed and cannot touch the real host. This bead captures the human-only steps to actually bring Postgres up on the production box.

## Task — run on the target host
Execute the scripts from vs-3ty and verify each acceptance criterion. Expect to generate and safely store one credential.

## Steps
1. `sudo ./setup.postgres.sh` (or `PG_PASSWORD=<chosen> sudo -E ./setup.postgres.sh` if you want to bring your own password).
2. Capture the generated password. Store in password manager under "Vacation Station / postgres / vs14". NEVER commit.
3. Verify service: `systemctl is-active postgresql` → "active"; `ss -tln | grep 5432` → listening on 127.0.0.1.
4. Verify DB: `PGPASSWORD=<pw> psql -h localhost -U vs14 -d vacation_station -c "select 1"` returns 1.
5. Copy `instances/vacation-station/config.toml.example` → `config.toml`, fill in real password. Do NOT commit the filled version (add to `.gitignore` if not already).
6. Confirm pg_hba.conf is localhost-only (Ubuntu default). Confirm port 5432 is NOT exposed publicly (`sudo ufw status` or cloud firewall console).
7. Enable backup timer: `sudo systemctl daemon-reload && sudo systemctl enable --now ss14-backup.timer`. Confirm `systemctl list-timers | grep ss14-backup` shows next run.
8. Run a manual backup once to prime retention dir: `sudo /opt/vacation-station/ops/postgres/backup.sh`. Confirm output file appears in `/var/backups/vacation-station/`.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `systemctl is-active postgresql` → active
- [ ] `psql -U vs14 -d vacation_station` connects successfully
- [ ] `vs14` password stored in password manager (not in repo, not in shell history)
- [ ] `instances/vacation-station/config.toml` present on host with real password, gitignored
- [ ] Port 5432 not exposed to public internet (firewall-verified)
- [ ] `ss14-backup.timer` enabled + active; at least one backup file exists
- [ ] Record completion + timestamp in this bead before closing

## Notes
Report any deviations from setup.postgres.sh (e.g. postgresql-17 instead of 16, different install layout) so we can PR fixes back into the script.

title: ci: benchmarks.yml fails — SSHs to upstream centcomm
description:
## Context
`.github/workflows/benchmarks.yml` runs on schedule (daily `0 8 * * *`).
Latest run: 2026-04-12 08:19 UTC, **failure**. Single failing step: "Run
script on centcomm".

## Root cause
```yaml
- name: Run script on centcomm
  uses: appleboy/ssh-action@master
  with:
    host: centcomm.spacestation14.io
    username: robust-benchmark-runner
    key: ${{ secrets.CENTCOMM_ROBUST_BENCHMARK_RUNNER_KEY }}
```
`centcomm.spacestation14.io` is Space Wizards' internal benchmark host;
`CENTCOMM_ROBUST_BENCHMARK_RUNNER_KEY` is their private SSH key. Neither
exist for our fork. This workflow was **upstream-only from the start** —
it only ever worked for the space-wizards/space-station-14 repo.

## Options
### Option A: delete
We're not running upstream's benchmark infrastructure. Pure noise to keep.
Every daily cron generates a failure email / notification. Cleanest.

### Option B: guard with `if: github.repository ==`
Mirror what `update-credits.yml` does — skip on forks. Upstream-sync stays
quiet because the file is preserved; local cron shows "skipped" not
"failed". Slightly more merge-friendly than deletion.

### Option C: wire to our own infrastructure
Stand up a VS14-equivalent of centcomm for perf tracking. Large, not now.

## Recommendation
**B** — tiny change (one `if:` line), no merge conflicts on future
upstream-sync, benchmark infrastructure can be built later (would pair
with vs-2p3 / vs-13x's metrics pipeline).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `if: github.repository == 'space-wizards/space-station-14'` (or
      our own equivalent if we ever build it) added to the benchmark job
- [ ] Next scheduled run reports "skipped", not "failed"
- [ ] No notifications leak
